title: How to Use Intuition When Making Decisions About Your Health
meta_description: Stop second guessing your gut. Your body knows before your mind does. Learn how to trust that quiet voice when making health decisions. No fluff. Just real guidance.
og_title: How to Use Intuition When Making Decisions About Your Health
og_description: Stop second guessing your gut. Your body knows before your mind does. Learn how to trust that quiet voice when making health decisions. No fluff. Just real guidance.
body:
You've Been Taught to Ignore Your Best Health Advisor

Let me guess. You've had that gut feeling about a health decision before - a quiet whisper that said "don't take that medication" or "something's off with this doctor" - and you talked yourself out of it. You told yourself you were being dramatic. You wanted proof, data, a second opinion from someone with credentials. And maybe you got that proof, went against your gut, and ended up worse off. Does that land?

You're not crazy. You're not making things up. Your nervous system is picking up signals that most people's aren't. And when it comes to your health, those signals matter more than you've been led to believe.

I'm Kalesh, and I've spent years working with people who feel everything. People who walk into a room and know someone's sick before anyone says a word. People who get a sudden urge to call their mom and find out she fell that morning. People whose bodies react to things their minds haven't processed yet. And almost every single one of them has been told their intuition is "just anxiety" or "overthinking."

Here's what I need you to hear: your intuition isn't the enemy of good health decisions. It's the missing piece. The part that bridges what your doctor's tests can show and what your body actually knows. And in this article, I'm going to show you how to use it without losing your mind or ignoring medical advice.

"Your intuition isn't a replacement for medical expertise - it's the translator between your body's truth and the choices you make about it. When you ignore that voice, you're not being rational. You're being deaf to half the conversation."

Why Your Health Intuition Got Shut Down in the First Place

Think back. When you were a kid and you said "I don't feel good about that" or "something's wrong with Grandma," what happened? Chances are, an adult told you not to be silly. They said you were imagining things. They told you to trust the experts, trust the tests, trust the numbers.

And so you learned to distrust your own knowing. You learned that your body's signals were unreliable, that your feelings were inconvenient, that being "sensitive" was a flaw to overcome rather than a gift to develop.

But here's the thing - your body never stopped talking. It just started whispering because you stopped listening.

Health decisions are high-stakes. That's why we get scared and cling to external authority. We want someone else to be responsible. We want a blood test to tell us what to do. But blood tests can't tell you how a medication will feel in your system. MRIs can't tell you if a treatment aligns with your deeper healing path. Only you can know that. And you do know it - you've just been trained to doubt it.

The Difference Between Intuition and Fear (This Matters More Than You Think)

This is where most people get stuck. "How do I know it's intuition and not just my anxiety acting up?" Right?! I hear this question every single week.

Here's the breakdown:

  • Fear feels tight, urgent, and reactive. It says "do something NOW" or "this is an emergency" even when nothing's actually happening. Fear is loud, repetitive, and comes with physical tension - clenched jaw, shallow breathing, racing heart.
  • Intuition feels calm, clear, and certain. It doesn't beg or plead. It just knows. It might come as a single word, a feeling of "yes" or "no," or a quiet knowing that doesn't need to justify itself. Intuition doesn't need you to act immediately - it just needs you to pay attention.

Here's a practical test: when you're trying to make a health decision, sit with it for 24 hours. If it's fear, the feeling will get louder and more demanding. If it's intuition, the knowing will stay steady or get quieter, but it won't change. Intuition doesn't panic. It doesn't need to.

Another way to tell: fear focuses on what you might lose. Intuition focuses on what's actually true. Fear says "what if this treatment doesn't work?" Intuition says "this treatment doesn't feel right for me." See the difference? One is a question rooted in lack. The other is a statement rooted in knowing.

How to Actually Hear Your Body's Health Signals

You can't use intuition you can't hear. And most of us have so much noise in our heads - doctor's opinions, family advice, internet research, worst-case scenarios - that we can't find the signal. So let's clear the static.

Step one: stop all input for 10 minutes. No phone, no people, no books, no podcasts. Just you and your breath. Close your eyes and put your hand on the part of your body that's involved in the decision. If it's a medication for your stomach, put your hand on your belly. If it's a surgery for your knee, put your hand on your knee. Just rest it there and breathe.

Step two: ask one question. Not ten. One. "What do I need to know about this?" or "Is this right for me?" Don't force an answer. Just ask and wait. You might get a word, a feeling, a memory, or nothing at all. That's fine. The point is to create space for your body to speak.

Step three: write down whatever comes. Don't judge it. Don't analyze it. Just write. Even if it seems stupid or irrelevant. Your intuition often speaks in symbols and sensations, not in complete sentences. Give it room to show up however it wants.

I recommend keeping a dedicated health intuition journal. Nothing fancy - a spiral notebook works. Date every entry. Over time, you'll start to see patterns. You'll notice that certain feelings always mean "yes" and certain physical sensations always mean "no." That's your personal body language. Learn it.

Using Intuition Alongside Medical Facts (Not Against Them)

This is the part that scares people. They think using intuition means rejecting medicine. Nothing could be further from the truth. Your intuition is meant to work with medical information, not replace it.

Think of it this way: medical tests and doctor's opinions give you the map. Your intuition tells you which path is yours. The map says there are three possible treatments for your condition. Your intuition says "this one feels aligned, this one feels heavy, and this one feels neutral." That's not rejecting medicine - that's personalizing it.

I've worked with countless people who ignored their gut about a medication their doctor prescribed. They took it anyway because the doctor said it was "the standard protocol." And they ended up with side effects the doctor didn't warn them about. Not because the doctor was wrong - but because the doctor couldn't feel what that specific medication would do in that specific body. Only you can feel that.

So here's what I recommend: when your doctor gives you options, don't decide right away. Say "I need some time to think about this." Take the information home. Sit with it in your body. Ask your intuition for guidance. Then make your decision from a place of both knowledge and knowing.

If you're looking for a book that bridges the gap between intuitive knowing and practical health decisions, I recommend "The Body Keeps the Score" by Bessel van der Kolk. It's not an intuition book per se, but it explains how your body stores information your mind hasn't processed yet - which is exactly the mechanism your health intuition runs on. See on Amazon (paid link).

When Your Intuition Says Something Different From Your Doctor

This is the hard one. You're sitting in the exam room. Your doctor, who has years of training and a wall full of certificates, tells you that you need a certain procedure or medication. And your gut says "no." What do you do?

First, don't panic. You're not in a battle between your intuition and your doctor. You're in a conversation. And conversations require both parties to speak.

Second, get curious. Ask your doctor questions. "What are the alternatives?" "What does the research say about long-term outcomes?" "What would happen if we waited?" Sometimes your intuition is saying "no" because there's a better option you haven't explored yet. Give the doctor a chance to provide more information.

Third, trust that you can walk away. You have the right to get a second opinion. You have the right to say "I need to think about this." You have the right to choose a different path, even if it's less conventional. Your body is yours. No one else lives in it.

But here's the part nobody tells you: sometimes your intuition says "no" because you're scared, not because the treatment is wrong. That's why the 24-hour rule matters. Give yourself time to separate fear from knowing. If after a day of sitting with it, the "no" is still clear and calm, listen to it. If it's gotten louder and more anxious, it might be fear masquerading as intuition.

I've seen people save their own lives by trusting their gut when every expert told them otherwise. And I've seen people suffer because they ignored that quiet voice. You are the expert on your own body. Don't forget that.

Building Your Health Intuition Muscle (It's a Practice, Not a Magic Trick)

Intuition isn't something you either have or don't have. It's a skill. And like any skill, it gets stronger with use. Here are three practices that will dramatically improve your ability to hear and trust your health intuition.

Practice one: check in with your body three times a day. Morning, noon, and night. Just pause for 30 seconds and ask "how am I really feeling?" Not what you think you should feel. Not what your diagnosis says you should feel. What you actually feel. This builds the connection between your conscious mind and your body's wisdom.

Practice two: start with low-stakes decisions. Don't start with "should I have surgery?" Start with "should I eat this or that for lunch?" Or "should I take a walk or rest?" Practice listening to your body about small things. When you get good at that, you'll trust it with big things.

Practice three: review your hits and misses. Once a week, look back at the intuitive nudges you got. Which ones were right? Which ones were wrong? You'll learn your intuition's language this way. Maybe your "yes" feels like warmth in your chest and your "no" feels like a knot in your stomach. Maybe your intuition speaks through dreams or through sudden thoughts that pop into your head. Learn your dialect.

If you want to go deeper into developing your intuitive sensitivity, I highly recommend "The Gift of Sensitivity" by Elena V. K. Smith. It's written specifically for people whose nervous systems pick up more than the average person. It will help you understand why you feel so much and how to use it instead of being overwhelmed by it. See on Amazon (paid link).

What to Do When Your Intuition Is Silent

Sometimes you sit down to ask your body a question and you get nothing. Dead air. No feeling, no word, no knowing. Just blankness. What then?

First, don't force it. Forcing creates static. If you're getting nothing, step away. Go for a walk. Take a shower. Do something that doesn't require thinking. Often the answer comes when you're not looking for it - in the shower, while driving, right before you fall asleep.

Second, look at your environment. Are you exhausted? Stressed? Overwhelmed? Your intuition is always speaking, but if you're running on empty, you can't hear it. Sometimes the most intuitive thing you can do is rest. Give yourself permission to not decide until you've recovered.

Third, use a different channel. If you can't feel an answer, try writing. Start a page with "what I need to know about this health decision is..." and just write without thinking. Or try drawing. Or moving your body. Intuition doesn't only come through feelings. It comes through images, sensations, memories, and even other people's words. Stay open to different forms of receiving.

And sometimes, the silence itself is the answer. Silence can mean "not yet" or "you already know" or "this isn't the right question." Trust that too.

Why Your Health Intuition Gets Louder When You're Sick

There's a pattern I see over and over: people say their intuition gets stronger when they're sick or in crisis. That's not random. When your body is under stress, it strips away the noise. All the social conditioning, the "shoulds," the other people's opinions - they fall away. What's left is raw knowing.

This is why so many people report having a "knowing" about their diagnosis before the tests confirm it. Or why they suddenly know which treatment is right for them when their life depends on it. Crisis clarifies. It cuts through the bullshit.

But you don't have to wait for a crisis. You can cultivate that clarity now, when things are relatively calm. Because the more you practice listening when it's easy, the more you'll be able to hear when it's hard.

And here's something nobody tells you: your health intuition isn't just about avoiding bad outcomes. It's also about choosing the path that actually heals you. Two people can have the same diagnosis and the same treatment, and one gets better while the other doesn't. Why? Because healing isn't just physical. It's energetic, emotional, and spiritual. Your intuition knows what your body needs to actually heal, not just what the protocol says.

If you're dealing with a chronic condition and want to understand how your sensitivity plays into your health, pick up "When the Body Says No" by Gabor Maté. It's a deep look at how suppressed intuition and ignored body signals contribute to illness. It might change everything you think about your health. See on Amazon (paid link).

Your Health Intuition Is a Relationship, Not a Tool

You can't use intuition like a hammer. You can't demand answers on command. Intuition is a relationship - with your body, with your deeper self, with the part of you that knows things before your brain catches up. And relationships require trust, time, and consistency.

So stop treating your intuition like a malfunctioning GPS. Start treating it like a wise friend who's been with you your whole life. That friend has been trying to get your attention. It's time to start listening.

You don't have to get it perfect. You don't have to never doubt yourself again. You just have to start. One decision at a time. One check-in at a time. One quiet moment of listening at a time.

Your body knows. It's always known. The question is: are you ready to hear it?

If you're ready to go deeper into developing your intuitive skills for health and every other area of your life, take our free assessment to discover your intuitive strengths and blind spots. It'll show you exactly where to focus your practice. Click here to start the assessment.
